h1Title: Poliomyelitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Poliomyelitis is an acute enteroviral infection caused by poliovirus that destroys motor neurons in the spinal cord and brainstem, producing irreversible flaccid paralysis in roughly 1 in every 200 infections. The Global Polio Eradication Initiative, launched in 1988 when wild poliovirus paralysed more than 350,000 children per year across 125 countries, has reduced that burden by more than 99% through oral and inactivated vaccine campaigns. Wild poliovirus type 2 was certified eradicated in 2015; type 3 in 2019. As of 2024, type 1 circulates in Pakistan and Afghanistan, while vaccine-derived poliovirus (cVDPV) outbreaks occur episodically in under-vaccinated communities worldwide. There is no antiviral treatment; vaccination is the only tool that prevents paralysis.

keyStats.prevalence: Wild poliovirus cases: 12 in 2023 globally (WHO); vaccine-derived poliovirus (cVDPV) cases: 328 in 2023 across Africa, Asia, and sporadically in high-income countries with low vaccination coverage
keyStats.demographics: Children under 5 carry the highest risk; adults are susceptible without prior vaccination; paralysis more common in older children than infants
keyStats.avgAge: Peak risk in children under 5 in endemic regions; paralysis rate and severity increase with age at infection
keyStats.globalCases: At peak (1988): approximately 350,000 paralytic cases per year. By 2023: fewer than 350 combined wild + cVDPV cases globally

definition: Poliomyelitis (ICD-10: A80.9) is an acute infection of the central nervous system caused by poliovirus, an RNA virus of the Picornaviridae family (genus Enterovirus). Three serotypes exist: wild poliovirus types 1 (WPV1), 2 (WPV2, eradicated 2015), and 3 (WPV3, eradicated 2019), plus circulating vaccine-derived polioviruses (cVDPV) that emerge from rare reversion of the attenuated oral vaccine strains. Poliovirus enters via the fecal-oral or, less commonly, oral-oral route; replicates in the oropharyngeal and intestinal mucosa; achieves viremia; and, in approximately 1-2% of infections, invades the central nervous system. Within the CNS, the virus has a selective tropism for large anterior horn motor neurons and neurons in the brainstem motor nuclei, which it destroys by lytic infection. Clinically, more than 95% of infections are asymptomatic (abortive), 4-8% produce a non-specific febrile illness (minor illness), about 1% produces aseptic meningitis without paralysis (non-paralytic poliomyelitis), and 0.1-2% produce the feared acute flaccid paralysis (paralytic poliomyelitis). Bulbar involvement, when respiratory and swallowing motor neurons are destroyed, creates the most life-threatening presentation. Poliomyelitis is managed by infectious disease, neurology, and rehabilitation medicine specialists, and is a WHO-notifiable disease requiring immediate public-health reporting.

typesClassification.description: Occurs in approximately 95% of infections. Fever, malaise, sore throat, and headache lasting 1-3 days with no neurological involvement. Patients are highly infectious via fecal-oral route but the infection is clinically unrecognized.
Approximately 1-5% of infections. Meningeal signs with CSF pleocytosis, stiff neck, and headache after the minor illness phase. All symptoms resolve fully over 1-2 weeks; no paralysis.

The most common paralytic form, affecting limb muscles through destruction of lumbar or cervical anterior horn cells. Asymmetric, sudden, flaccid lower-motor-neuron paralysis with absent deep tendon reflexes and muscle wasting. Leg involvement predominates.

Brainstem motor nuclei and spinal cord are both affected, causing pharyngeal, facial, and respiratory muscle weakness combined with limb paralysis. Requires mechanical ventilation; the highest mortality form.

Cranial nerve nuclei (IX, X, XI, XII) are selectively destroyed, causing dysphagia, dysphonia, facial weakness, and respiratory failure. Accounts for 2% of paralytic cases but carries the highest short-term mortality from aspiration and respiratory failure.

primarySymptoms: Sudden asymmetric flaccid paralysis of one or more limbs, typically appearing 3-8 days after the febrile prodrome and progressing over 24-48 hours to maximum weakness.
Loss of deep tendon reflexes in the affected limb(s) — distinguishes lower motor neuron (poliovirus) from upper motor neuron causes of paralysis.
Fever, severe headache, nausea, vomiting, and neck and back stiffness 1-2 days before paralysis onset — the preparalytic or meningitic phase.
Severe myalgia and muscle spasm, particularly in the neck, back, and limbs that precede and accompany the onset of paralysis.
Asymmetric weakness that is proximal-dominant (hip and shoulder girdle muscles more affected than distal muscles), in contrast to Guillain-Barré syndrome which is typically distal and symmetric.
Marked muscle wasting appearing within 1-2 weeks in the affected limb — rapid atrophy from lower motor neuron denervation.
Dysphagia, nasal regurgitation, and change in voice quality when cranial nerve nuclei IX and X are involved in bulbar poliomyelitis.
Respiratory distress with paradoxical abdominal movement and use of accessory muscles when thoracic and diaphragmatic motor neurons are affected.
Autonomic disturbances including profuse sweating, labile blood pressure, and urinary retention in severe spinal and bulbospinal disease.
earlyWarningSigns: Fever with unusual irritability and complaint of severe back or neck pain in a child from an endemic region or a community with low vaccination coverage.
New onset of limb pain or extreme sensitivity to touch (hyperesthesia) in a febrile child — may precede flaccid paralysis by 12-24 hours.
Any acute flaccid paralysis (AFP) in a child under 15 is an immediate WHO reportable event regardless of the suspected cause, as it triggers poliovirus investigation.
Nasal voice, drooling, or difficulty swallowing in a febrile child — early bulbar signs that may precede respiratory failure within hours.
emergencySigns: Progressive respiratory distress, declining oxygen saturation, and paradoxical breathing in paralytic poliomyelitis — requires immediate intubation and mechanical ventilation before arrest.
Bulbar palsy with inability to swallow saliva or maintain airway — aspiration pneumonia and asphyxia are immediate risks requiring airway protection.
Rapidly ascending paralysis reaching the thorax within 24 hours of limb paralysis — bulbospinal involvement is likely; ICU with ventilator capability is essential.
High fever with meningism plus acute flaccid paralysis in an unvaccinated child — public health notification and diagnostic workup must proceed immediately without waiting for confirmation.

causes.description: Poliovirus is shed in the feces for 3-6 weeks after infection and is transmitted via contaminated water, food, or hands. Person-to-person transmission is efficient in settings with inadequate sanitation. WPV1 remains endemic in Afghanistan and Pakistan as of 2024, with importation risk globally.

The attenuated oral poliovirus vaccine (OPV) can revert toward neurovirulence during replication in the gut and circulate in under-vaccinated communities over time. cVDPV2 is the most common type in contemporary outbreaks in Africa and Asia; it behaves clinically identically to wild poliovirus. Paradoxically, OPV use is both the cause of cVDPV and the tool for outbreak response.

A rare adverse event (1 per 2.4 million doses) where the OPV strain reverts enough neurovirulence to paralyse the vaccinee or an immunologically naive close contact. Risk is entirely eliminated by switching to inactivated poliovirus vaccine (IPV), which cannot cause VAPP.

Individuals with primary B-cell immunodeficiencies (hypogammaglobulinemia, combined immunodeficiency) can excrete poliovirus or OPV-derived strains for years after vaccination, serving as a persistent reservoir. These individuals may develop late-onset progressive polio.

Poliovirus spreads explosively in populations where less than 80-85% of children have received three or more doses of polio vaccine. Conflict zones, displacement camps, and communities with vaccine hesitancy are the most vulnerable. Every susceptible child in the world is an opportunity for a paralytic case as long as the virus circulates anywhere.

riskFactors.description: The dominant risk factor. Three doses of IPV or OPV confer more than 99% protection against paralytic disease. Children with fewer than 3 doses are susceptible. Adults who received no childhood polio vaccine are also at risk if exposed.

riskFactors.factor: Age under 5 years in endemic areas
riskFactors.description: Pre-immune young children in endemic or outbreak-affected communities account for the overwhelming majority of paralytic cases.

riskFactors.factor: Travel to or residence in poliovirus-endemic regions
riskFactors.description: WPV1 persists in Pakistan and Afghanistan; cVDPV outbreaks occur in parts of sub-Saharan Africa. Imported cases from travelers are documented in countries that had been polio-free for decades.
Poliovirus survives in sewage for weeks; communities drinking untreated surface water or practicing open defecation sustain fecal-oral transmission chains even with vaccination campaigns.
Individuals with X-linked agammaglobulinemia or CVID cannot produce vaccine-induced neutralizing antibodies and are at risk for both paralytic polio and prolonged viral excretion.

riskFactors.factor: Pregnancy at time of wild poliovirus exposure
riskFactors.description: Pregnant women have slightly higher risk of paralytic disease; poliovirus can be transmitted to the neonate in utero or during delivery, though congenital paralytic polio is rare.

riskFactors.factor: Intramuscular injections or vigorous exercise during the preparalytic phase
riskFactors.description: Intramuscular injections into a limb during the viremic phase of poliovirus infection increase the risk that that limb is paralyzed (provocation poliomyelitis); vigorous exercise also correlates with increased paralysis risk.

affectedDemographics: Children under 5 in areas of active WPV1 or cVDPV transmission (Pakistan, Afghanistan, parts of sub-Saharan Africa) carry the highest incidence of paralytic disease.
Unvaccinated adults returning from endemic regions have documented paralytic cases in Western Europe and North America.
Displaced and refugee populations in conflict zones often have interrupted vaccination schedules and are at elevated risk during outbreaks.
Individuals with primary B-cell immunodeficiency face prolonged poliovirus excretion and late-onset progressive polio regardless of vaccination history.
Environmental surveillance (sewage PCR) in New York City and London detected poliovirus in 2022, signaling transmission in under-vaccinated urban communities.
diagnosisOverview: Acute poliomyelitis is a clinical and public health emergency. The hallmark presentation of acute flaccid paralysis (AFP) in a febrile child or unvaccinated adult in an at-risk epidemiological setting mandates immediate WHO notification, specimen collection, and isolation. Confirmation requires virological testing: two stool samples collected 24-48 hours apart within 14 days of paralysis onset are the gold standard; poliovirus isolation in cell culture followed by intratypic differentiation separates wild poliovirus, OPV-derived, and cVDPV. Serum and CSF neutralization antibodies are less sensitive when an anamnestic response complicates interpretation. CSF analysis typically shows mild lymphocytic pleocytosis (10-200 cells/µL), mildly elevated protein, and normal glucose — an aseptic meningitis pattern — in the preparalytic phase. MRI of the spinal cord demonstrates T2 signal hyperintensity and enhancement in the anterior horn (butterfly pattern) in acute paralytic disease, indistinguishable from other acute flaccid myelitis causes including enterovirus D68. Nerve conduction studies and electromyography (NCS/EMG) within 4-6 weeks distinguish anterior horn cell injury (reduced amplitude CMAP with normal conduction velocity; denervation on EMG) from demyelinating neuropathy. Residual paralysis at 60 days after onset is the WHO epidemiological criterion for confirming paralytic poliomyelitis.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool poliovirus culture (two samples, 24-48 hours apart)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard to isolate and identify poliovirus type; distinguishes wild poliovirus from vaccine-derived strains
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Stool samples collected within 14 days of paralysis onset are shipped cold to a WHO-accredited laboratory. Cell culture results return in 2-3 weeks. Two samples on consecutive days substantially increase sensitivity to over 90%.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports diagnosis of aseptic meningitis phase; excludes other causes such as bacterial meningitis or Guillain-Barré syndrome
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard lumbar puncture procedure; 15-30 minutes. CSF in polio shows 10-200 lymphocytes/µL, mildly elevated protein, and normal glucose. Performed under sterile conditions; mild back soreness for 24 hours afterward.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates anterior horn signal abnormality (T2 hyperintensity, gadolinium enhancement) confirming myelitis pattern
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in an MRI scanner. Contrast agent injected via a small IV cannula. Anterior horn or ventral grey matter enhancement distinguishes polio and acute flaccid myelitis from Guillain-Barré (normal cord) and spinal cord infarction.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents anterior horn cell injury pattern: reduced CMAP amplitude, normal conduction velocity, denervation fibrillations at 3-4 weeks — distinguishes polio from Guillain-Barré demyelinating pattern
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-invasive electrical impulse test with small surface electrodes plus thin EMG needles. Mildly uncomfortable. Most informative 4-6 weeks after onset; results within the same session.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects type-specific neutralizing antibodies in paired acute and convalescent sera; a 4-fold rise confirms recent infection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw at two time points (acute and 2-4 weeks later). Less useful in vaccinated individuals where baseline titers are already high.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects poliovirus RNA in community sewage before or in the absence of clinical cases; key tool for outbreak detection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A population-level public-health test, not a clinical test performed on individual patients. Positive results trigger immediate clinical surveillance and vaccination campaigns in the affected area.

treatmentOverview: There is no antiviral treatment that eradicates poliovirus from the CNS or reverses motor neuron destruction. Management is entirely supportive and rehabilitation-focused. In the acute paralytic phase, the priority is airway protection and respiratory support: patients with bulbar or respiratory involvement require immediate assessment by critical care and, if vital capacity falls below 15-20 mL/kg or there is paradoxical breathing, intubation and mechanical ventilation. The historic iron lung (negative-pressure ventilation) has been replaced by modern positive-pressure mechanical ventilators and, where feasible, non-invasive ventilation. Adequate pain management for the severe myalgia and spasm of the preparalytic phase uses analgesics and, historically, hot packs — a rehabilitative technique that reduces muscle guarding. Bed rest during the acute febrile-paralytic phase is essential; vigorous exercise or unnecessary intramuscular injections at this stage worsen motor neuron destruction. Nutritional support — enteral via nasogastric tube in bulbar disease — prevents the cachexia that worsens muscle recovery. Physical therapy begins as soon as fever resolves: passive range-of-motion exercises maintain joint mobility and prevent contractures during the flaccid phase; active exercises and hydrotherapy are introduced as residual strength is assessed. Orthotics, splints, and callipers preserve functional posture and permit ambulation in patients with partial recovery. Long-term rehabilitation with orthopaedic surgical interventions (tendon transfers, osteotomies) improves functional outcomes years after the acute episode. Vaccination of all close contacts and community-level outbreak response vaccination are simultaneous public-health priorities.

medicalTreatments.name: Mechanical ventilation (positive pressure)
medicalTreatments.description: Life-saving respiratory support for bulbar and bulbospinal poliomyelitis. Modern ICU ventilators have replaced the iron lung. Non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (CPAP, BiPAP) used in moderate impairment; invasive intubation and mechanical ventilation for rapidly declining respiratory function.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Survival rates for paralytic polio have risen from under 50% in the iron-lung era to 80-90% in modern ICU settings; survival depends on duration of ventilator dependence

medicalTreatments.name: Analgesics and non-pharmacological pain management
medicalTreatments.description: Paracetamol (acetaminophen) and NSAIDs for fever and myalgia during the prodromal phase. Warm moist hot packs applied to affected muscles reduce spasm and pain; a technique pioneered by Sister Kenny in the 1940s and still used in rehabilitation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective symptom relief; no evidence any analgesic alters the paralytic outcome

medicalTreatments.name: Enteral nutritional support via nasogastric tube
medicalTreatments.description: Nasogastric tube feeding for patients with bulbar weakness who cannot swallow safely. High-protein, high-calorie formula (1.2-1.5 g protein/kg/day) supports muscle anabolism during recovery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Maintains nutritional status; prevents aspiration pneumonia from unsafe oral feeding in dysphagia

medicalTreatments.name: Physical therapy and hydrotherapy
medicalTreatments.description: Passive range-of-motion from day 1 prevents contractures; active strengthening begins once fever resolves. Warm-water hydrotherapy reduces muscle tone and pain, allows early weight-bearing in partially affected limbs.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Systematic review evidence supports physiotherapy for preventing contractures; functional outcomes substantially better in patients with early intensive rehabilitation

medicalTreatments.name: Orthotic devices (callipers, ankle-foot orthoses, splints)
medicalTreatments.description: Customized braces maintain alignment, prevent foot drop and equinus contracture, and permit weight-bearing ambulation during the recovery phase and long-term.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Allow community ambulation in 70-80% of patients with partial motor recovery who would otherwise be non-ambulatory

surgicalOptions.name: Tendon transfer surgery
surgicalOptions.description: Functional tendons from unparalyzed muscles are surgically rerouted to replace the actions of paralyzed ones. Most commonly performed for foot drop (tibialis posterior transfer) or hand paralysis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Restoration of functional movement in 70-85% of selected cases; best results when performed 12-24 months after maximal paralysis with stable muscle groups

surgicalOptions.name: Corrective osteotomy for limb deformity
surgicalOptions.description: Bone realignment surgery to correct fixed joint deformities (equinus foot, scoliosis, hip dislocation) that develop from long-standing muscle imbalance after paralytic polio.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Significant functional improvement in ambulatory status in 60-80% of children with fixed lower-limb deformities

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease specialist manages the acute diagnostic workup, public health notification, and contact vaccination; a neurologist confirms the anterior horn cell localization and excludes Guillain-Barré syndrome and other acute flaccid myelitis causes; a rehabilitation medicine physician leads the long-term functional recovery plan.
doctorSelectionGuide: During the acute phase, the nearest ICU with neurology and infectious disease capability is essential. For long-term rehabilitation, seek a team with paediatric physiatrist, orthopedic surgeon experienced in post-polio deformity, orthotist, and physiotherapist with neuromuscular expertise.
hospitalCriteria: ICU with mechanical ventilation for bulbar or respiratory involvement
Neurology service for acute flaccid paralysis workup and differential diagnosis
WHO-accredited laboratory or specimen shipping capability for stool poliovirus culture
Rehabilitation medicine unit for early physiotherapy and orthotics
Pediatric critical care for children under 15 with bulbospinal disease
keyFacilities: Regional WHO polio reference laboratories (specimen shipping and culture)
Pediatric neurology and infectious disease units
Tertiary rehabilitation centers with post-polio deformity surgery capability
Orthotics and prosthetics workshops for long-term device provision
preventionStrategies: Vaccinate with inactivated poliovirus vaccine (IPV) per national childhood schedule: at least three primary doses plus a booster; IPV cannot cause vaccine-associated paralysis.
Ensure booster vaccination for travelers to Pakistan, Afghanistan, or areas with active poliovirus circulation — some countries require proof of polio vaccination for entry.
Support community-wide supplementary immunization activities (SIAs) and national immunization days in outbreak-affected regions.
Improve water and sanitation infrastructure — piped water, treated sewage, and latrines interrupt fecal-oral transmission chains and protect unvaccinated individuals.

Report any acute flaccid paralysis in a child under 15 immediately to public health authorities — rapid specimen collection within 14 days of onset is the cornerstone of global eradication surveillance.
Screen immunodeficient individuals for poliovirus excretion and use IPV-only schedules for household contacts of immunodeficient patients to prevent VAPP.
lifestyleModifications: During the acute paralytic phase, rest affected limbs in neutral anatomical position with splints to prevent contracture.
Start passive range-of-motion exercises under physiotherapist guidance as soon as acute fever resolves.
Maintain adequate protein-rich nutrition throughout recovery to support denervated muscle metabolism and collateral reinnervation.
Avoid overexertion and fatigue in the recovering or post-polio patient; excessive exercise of partially denervated muscles worsens late motor unit loss (post-polio syndrome).
Attend regularly scheduled physiotherapy, orthopaedic, and neurology review appointments to monitor for late deterioration.
Pursue vocational rehabilitation and assistive technology (wheelchairs, orthoses) early to preserve community participation.

recommended: High-protein diet (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) from lean meat, fish, legumes, and dairy to support muscle repair and collateral reinnervation
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation to prevent disuse osteoporosis in paralyzed limbs
Iron-rich foods or supplementation if prolonged immobility or anorexia leads to anemia
Adequate caloric intake to prevent cachexia during prolonged rehabilitation
avoid: Obesity-promoting foods that increase mechanical load on weakened limbs
Excessive alcohol, which worsens peripheral neuropathy and motor recovery
Prolonged fasting or low-protein diets during recovery that impair muscle anabolism

exerciseGuidelines: During acute illness, complete bedrest and limb positioning is required. As fever resolves, gentle passive range-of-motion begins under therapist supervision. In the recovery phase, low-impact exercises in warm water (hydrotherapy) reduce pain and allow graduated weight-bearing. High-intensity exercise must be avoided in post-polio syndrome — overexertion accelerates motor unit loss. Walking with callipers or assistive devices is encouraged; swimming and cycling are preferred aerobic activities for long-term fitness without joint overloading.
dailyManagement: Inspect paralyzed limbs daily for pressure sores, contractures, and skin breakdown — denervated skin is fragile and heals poorly.
Reposition and turn bed-bound patients every 2 hours to prevent decubitus ulcers.
Perform prescribed passive and active exercises every day; inconsistent therapy leads to permanent contracture and functional loss.
Monitor respiratory status daily in patients with thoracic or bulbar involvement — vital capacity, oxygen saturation, and peak cough flow track early deterioration.
Ensure orthotic devices are correctly fitted and inspected weekly for pressure points.
prognosis: Overall prognosis depends on the degree of motor neuron destruction at onset. Approximately 50% of paralyzed muscle groups show some recovery within 6-12 months through collateral reinnervation and hypertrophy of surviving motor units; maximal recovery typically plateaus at 18-24 months. Children under 5 recover more muscle function than older patients. Limb paralysis from spinal polio: full or near-full recovery in roughly 30% of affected limbs; partial recovery in 40%; permanent severe weakness in 30%. Respiratory polio: those who survive acute respiratory failure with ventilatory support have 70-80% long-term survival but may remain ventilator-dependent. Post-polio syndrome develops in 25-40% of paralytic polio survivors 15-40 years later, causing new weakness, fatigue, and pain. Mortality in the acute phase: under 1% for spinal polio; up to 20-60% for untreated bulbar or bulbospinal disease. With full vaccination coverage, paralytic polio is entirely preventable.
recoveryTimeline: Acute paralytic phase: progresses over 24-48 hours; plateau within 5-10 days. Recovery phase: begins 4-8 weeks after onset; most recovery within first 12 months; maximal recovery plateau at 18-24 months. Long-term: post-polio syndrome may emerge 15-40 years later. Ventilator-dependent patients may wean over weeks to months with sustained respiratory muscle recovery.
complications: Permanent flaccid paralysis in 0.1-2% of all poliovirus infections — predominantly asymmetric leg weakness; the defining irreversible complication.
Respiratory failure from intercostal and diaphragmatic paralysis requiring long-term mechanical ventilation — affects patients with thoracic anterior horn involvement.

Post-polio syndrome: new progressive muscle weakness, fatigue, and pain appearing 15-40 years after acute paralytic polio in 25-40% of survivors; mechanisms include late motor neuron loss and overuse of surviving units.
Joint contractures, scoliosis, and limb length discrepancy from long-standing muscle imbalance — preventable with early physiotherapy and orthotics.
Aspiration pneumonia from bulbar palsy — the leading cause of acute death in non-ventilated bulbar polio.
Osteoporosis and pathological fractures in chronically paralyzed limbs due to disuse and reduced weight-bearing — requires calcium, vitamin D, and weight-bearing physiotherapy.

supportResources.name: WHO Global Polio Eradication Initiative
supportResources.description: Real-time global case counts, outbreak maps, vaccination strategies, and eradication program updates from the GPEI partnership.

supportResources.name: CDC Polio Information
supportResources.description: US vaccination schedules, travel guidance, post-polio syndrome information, and outbreak surveillance.

supportResources.name: Post-Polio Health International
supportResources.description: Patient support, education, and research resources for individuals living with polio sequelae and post-polio syndrome.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bacterial Meningitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bacterial meningitis causes fever, meningism, and altered consciousness but does not produce flaccid paralysis with anterior horn cell destruction. CSF in bacterial meningitis shows purulent pleocytosis with low glucose and positive Gram stain, whereas polio CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis with normal glucose. MRI in polio shows anterior horn signal; bacterial meningitis affects the meninges diffusely.

confusedWithConditions.name: Miliary Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tuberculous meningitis can cause cranial nerve palsies and rarely spinal cord involvement, but onset is subacute over weeks with prominent basilar meningeal enhancement on MRI, CSF culture positive for Mycobacterium tuberculosis, and no acute flaccid limb paralysis pattern. Polio presents acutely with flaccid paralysis, normal or near-normal CSF glucose, and anterior horn MRI signal.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leptospirosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leptospirosis causes fever, myalgia, and meningismus but no acute flaccid paralysis or anterior horn destruction. The hallmarks of leptospirosis are jaundice, renal failure, and conjunctival suffusion following freshwater exposure; serology confirms Leptospira infection, and treatment is penicillin or doxycycline rather than supportive rehabilitation.

relatedConditions.name: African Trypanosomiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another neglected tropical disease affecting the CNS and predominantly afflicting low-resource settings; shares the focus of global elimination programs.

relatedConditions.name: Miliary Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: TB can involve the CNS and cause acute neurological presentations requiring urgent treatment in the same resource-limited settings where polio occurs.

relatedConditions.name: Bacterial Meningitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Enters the differential of acute febrile neurological illness in children; CSF analysis and MRI distinguish poliovirus anterior horn disease from bacterial meningitis.

faqs.question: What is poliomyelitis?
faqs.answer: Poliomyelitis is a viral infection caused by poliovirus that destroys motor neurons in the spinal cord or brainstem, producing irreversible flaccid (floppy) paralysis. About 1 in 200 infections leads to paralysis; the rest cause only a mild febrile illness or no symptoms at all. There is no cure; vaccination is the only way to prevent paralysis.

faqs.question: Is polio still a threat today?
faqs.answer: Wild poliovirus type 1 still circulates in Pakistan and Afghanistan, causing 12 confirmed cases in 2023. Vaccine-derived poliovirus causes outbreaks in under-vaccinated communities globally, including detections in sewage in New York and London in 2022. One unvaccinated traveler or a gap in childhood coverage can restart transmission.

faqs.question: How does poliovirus cause paralysis?
faqs.answer: Poliovirus enters via the mouth, replicates in the intestines, enters the bloodstream, and in about 1% of infections invades the spinal cord. It selectively destroys large anterior horn motor neurons. When enough neurons serving a muscle group die, that muscle cannot contract, producing permanent flaccid paralysis.

faqs.question: What are the signs of polio paralysis?
faqs.answer: Sudden asymmetric flaccid weakness of one or more limbs appearing 3-8 days after fever. The affected limb is floppy (not stiff), deep tendon reflexes are absent, and the muscles waste rapidly. Leg involvement is most common. Bulbar polio adds difficulty swallowing, voice change, and respiratory distress.

faqs.question: How is polio diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Two stool samples collected within 14 days of paralysis onset are the gold standard; poliovirus isolated in cell culture confirms the diagnosis and identifies the strain. CSF shows mild lymphocytic pleocytosis. MRI spinal cord shows anterior horn signal. Any acute flaccid paralysis in a child under 15 must be reported to WHO surveillance immediately.

faqs.question: Is there a treatment for polio?
faqs.answer: There is no antiviral drug that treats poliovirus infection. Management is supportive: mechanical ventilation for respiratory failure, pain relief, physiotherapy, and orthotics. Physical rehabilitation maximizes recovery of surviving motor neurons. Prevention through vaccination is the only way to stop paralysis from occurring.

faqs.question: What is the polio vaccine and how many doses are needed?
faqs.answer: Two vaccine types exist: inactivated poliovirus vaccine (IPV, injectable) and oral poliovirus vaccine (OPV). IPV cannot cause vaccine-associated paralysis and is preferred in polio-free countries. Most schedules require 3-4 doses in childhood for protection above 99%. Boosters are recommended for travelers to endemic areas.

faqs.question: Can vaccinated people still get polio?
faqs.answer: Vaccinated individuals with complete schedules are protected against paralysis in over 99% of cases. Rare breakthrough infections in immunocompromised individuals and vaccine-associated paralytic poliomyelitis (1 per 2.4 million OPV doses) can occur. IPV eliminates the vaccine-associated risk entirely.

faqs.question: What is post-polio syndrome?
faqs.answer: Post-polio syndrome is new progressive muscle weakness, fatigue, and pain that develops 15-40 years after acute paralytic polio in 25-40% of survivors. It is caused by gradual loss of the overworked surviving motor units. There is no treatment that halts progression; energy conservation, orthoses, and non-fatiguing exercise manage symptoms.

faqs.question: Can adults get polio?
faqs.answer: Yes. Unvaccinated adults who are exposed to poliovirus or vaccine-derived poliovirus are at risk. Adults tend to develop more severe paralysis than young children. In 2022, an unvaccinated young adult in New York developed paralytic polio after likely sewage exposure, highlighting the risk in under-vaccinated communities.

faqs.question: How long does polio paralysis last?
faqs.answer: Paralysis is permanent in muscles whose motor neurons were destroyed. Partial recovery occurs in the first 12-18 months through collateral reinnervation of surviving neurons. About 30% of affected muscle groups recover fully; 40% recover partially; 30% remain severely weak. Maximal recovery is reached by 24 months after onset.

faqs.question: Is polio contagious?
faqs.answer: Yes. Poliovirus is highly contagious via the fecal-oral route and, to a lesser extent, oral-oral droplets. An infected person sheds virus in the stool for 3-6 weeks. In under-vaccinated populations, one case typically generates 10-100 additional infections, most of which are asymptomatic.

faqs.question: What countries still have polio?
faqs.answer: As of 2024, wild poliovirus type 1 is endemic only in Pakistan and Afghanistan. Vaccine-derived poliovirus outbreaks occur intermittently in parts of sub-Saharan Africa, Pakistan, and have been detected in sewage in the US and UK in 2022, indicating undetected transmission in under-vaccinated communities.

faqs.question: Does polio affect the brain?
faqs.answer: Poliovirus primarily destroys spinal cord and brainstem motor neurons, not the brain cortex. Bulbar polio destroys cranial nerve nuclei causing swallowing difficulty and respiratory failure, which can cause secondary brain injury from hypoxia. Cognitive function is typically preserved in survivors of paralytic polio.

faqs.question: What should I do if I haven't been vaccinated against polio?
faqs.answer: Adults who have never been vaccinated should receive IPV as a 3-dose primary series. Adults who received only OPV in childhood can receive one IPV booster for added protection before travel to endemic areas. Check with a travel medicine clinic for current country-specific vaccine requirements.

metaDescription: Poliomyelitis (polio): poliovirus destroys motor neurons causing irreversible paralysis. Symptoms, vaccine types, diagnosis, treatment and prognosis reviewed.
